narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 60% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, LSAT score of 40, Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles both at 3, middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average undergraduate GPA is 3.24, and typical Year 3 deciles are around 5.91, so your profile stands out as lower in these key academic areas compared to those who succeeded. The three most influential factors in this decision were your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and Year 3 grade decile, in that order of impact. Your strong LSAT performance was the top positive factor, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass by showing solid reasoning and analytical skills that often correlate with bar success. However, your undergraduate GPA had a notable negative pull, signaling to the model a weaker foundation in sustained academic performance relative to admitted students. Similarly, your Year 3 decile worked against you, as it suggested challenges in ranking higher among peers later in your studies, unlike the stronger showings from those who passed. Your middle family income and full-time status aligned reasonably well with successful students—full-time is the norm at 93% among passers, and middle income is common—but they didn't outweigh the academic concerns. Overall, while your LSAT gives you a real edge, the model saw the GPA and later decile as stronger signals of risk for bar failure, tipping the balance toward rejection. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, just a data-driven estimate to help the school select applicants most likely to thrive. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to build on your strength there, or focus on boosting your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses or targeted academic prep to shift future predictions in your favor. You might also explore programs with holistic reviews or appeal with evidence of recent improvements, as many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps. Keep pushing—your LSAT shows you have the aptitude, and with targeted steps, you can change the story for your next application.
